name: zig
description: Idiomatic Zig conventions, allocator patterns, error handling, comptime, testing, and build system practices

decisions.decision: Target the latest stable Zig release; set minimum_zig_version in build.zig.zon
decisions.rationale: Zig is pre-1.0 with breaking changes between minor versions. Targeting the latest stable ensures access to critical fixes and API improvements. minimum_zig_version documents compatibility (advisory only — not yet enforced by the compiler).
decisions.details: Check https://ziglang.org/download/ for the current stable release. Use master builds only for experimentation, never for production. The 0.14 to 0.15 migration was extremely breaking (I/O overhaul, ArrayList API changes, usingnamespace removal) — expect similar breaks until 1.0.

decisions.decision: Follow the official Zig style guide: PascalCase for types and type-returning functions, camelCase for regular functions, snake_case for variables and fields
decisions.rationale: Zig naming conventions encode semantic meaning. When you see a PascalCase function name, you immediately know it returns a type. This is a powerful signal embedded in naming that the community universally follows.
decisions.details:
Types (struct, enum, union, error): PascalCase (Point, Color, FileError).
Functions returning a type: PascalCase (fn ArrayList(comptime T: type) type).
Regular functions: camelCase (fn isSigned(), fn readByte()).
Variables, fields, constants: snake_case (var my_count, const max_size).
Namespace structs (zero fields): snake_case binding (const std = @import("std")).
Files exposing a type: PascalCase (Foo.zig, HttpClient.zig).
Regular files: snake_case (main.zig, root.zig, build_utils.zig).

zig fmt enforces whitespace/structure but NOT naming — naming is enforced by convention and review.

decisions.decision: Use explicit error sets for public APIs, inferred error sets for internals; errdefer for all partial cleanup
decisions.rationale: Explicit error sets document what can go wrong and enable exhaustive handling at call sites. Inferred error sets reduce boilerplate in internal code. errdefer is essential for correct cleanup when functions allocate multiple resources — it only runs when the function returns an error.
decisions.details:
Use try to propagate errors to the caller. Use catch for custom handling. Use errdefer for cleanup that should only happen on error path.

Patterns:
- errdefer allocator.free(memory) — free on error
- errdefer |err| log.err("failed: {}", .{err}) — log error context at origin
- errdefer comptime unreachable — compile-time assert that error path is impossible

Anti-patterns: catch unreachable unless mathematically proven impossible, catch {} silently swallowing errors, over-broad error sets that lose specificity.

decisions.decision: Always pass allocators as parameters; use DebugAllocator in development, ArenaAllocator for batch work, std.testing.allocator in tests
decisions.rationale: Explicit allocator injection is Zig's central design pattern — no hidden control flow. When you see an allocator parameter, the function will allocate. Different allocators serve different purposes: DebugAllocator detects leaks and UAF, ArenaAllocator enables efficient bulk allocation, std.testing.allocator automatically reports leaks.
decisions.details:
Allocator selection:
- Development: std.heap.DebugAllocator (leak/double-free/UAF detection)
- Production multithreaded: std.heap.SmpAllocator
- Batch work (parsers, request handlers): std.heap.ArenaAllocator
- Tests: std.testing.allocator (auto leak detection)
- Stack/embedded: std.heap.FixedBufferAllocator
- C interop: std.heap.c_allocator (requires -lc)

Every allocation must have a corresponding free. Use defer allocator.free(memory) immediately after allocation. For slices use alloc/free; for single items use create/destroy.

Anti-patterns: global allocators, calling individual free() on arena-allocated memory (it is a no-op), using page_allocator for small allocations.

decisions.decision: Use zig init for project scaffolding; build.zig for build instructions, build.zig.zon for package metadata
decisions.rationale: zig init creates the canonical project structure. build.zig is Zig code (not a DSL) providing full build system expressiveness. build.zig.zon is the package manifest with dependencies, version, and fingerprint.
decisions.details:
Standard layout:
  build.zig — build instructions
  build.zig.zon — package metadata (name, version, dependencies, fingerprint)
  src/main.zig — executable entry point
  src/root.zig — library root (delete if exe-only)
  src/Module.zig — PascalCase if the file exports a type
  .zig-cache/ — build cache (gitignore)
  zig-out/ — install output (gitignore)

Every .zig file IS a struct — @import returns the file's struct type. This enables 'files as namespaces' and 'files as types'.

Do not include 'zig' in the package name. Keep the name as a bare Zig identifier, max 32 bytes.

decisions.decision: Use build.zig.zon for dependencies with zig fetch --save; no central registry — use direct URLs
decisions.rationale: Zig's package manager is decentralized. Packages are distributed via Git URLs and tarballs. The hash in build.zig.zon IS the lock — no separate lockfile needed. Transitive dependencies are automatically resolved.

decisions.decision: Colocate tests with implementation; use std.testing.allocator for all test allocations; use descriptive test names
decisions.rationale: The standard library keeps tests in the same file as the code under test — this is the canonical pattern. std.testing.allocator automatically detects memory leaks after test completion. Descriptive names enable test filtering.
decisions.details:
Test declarations:
  test "descriptive name" { ... } — named, filterable with --test-filter
  test myFunction { ... } — doctest, appears in generated documentation
  test { ... } — unnamed, always runs regardless of filtering

Key assertions: expect(), expectEqual(), expectError(), expectEqualStrings(), expectEqualSlices().

Use std.testing.allocator for ALL test allocations. Skip tests with return error.SkipZigTest when a test cannot run on the current platform.

Run: zig build test (via build system), zig test src/main.zig (single file), zig build test -- --test-filter "name" (filtered).

decisions.decision: Use comptime for generics, type introspection, and compile-time validation; never for I/O or side effects
decisions.rationale: Comptime is Zig's single mechanism replacing macros, templates, and generics. It is hermetic, reproducible, and cacheable — no I/O or side effects allowed. This makes builds deterministic.
decisions.details:
Key patterns:
- Generic types: fn ArrayList(comptime T: type) type { return struct { ... }; }
- Type introspection: @typeInfo(), @FieldType(), @tagName(), @field()
- Loop unrolling: inline for over comptime-known sequences
- Compile-time validation: comptime assert() for assumptions
- Configuration: feature flags and protocol definitions at zero runtime cost
- Type generation: @Type() to build struct/union/enum types dynamically

Limitations by design: no I/O at comptime (use build.zig for build-time code generation), no dynamic code generation or string mixins, no custom syntax extension.

Anti-pattern: using comptime when runtime would suffice (wastes compile time).

decisions.decision: Use ReleaseSafe for production builds; rely on defer/errdefer for cleanup; understand that Zig lacks compile-time borrow checking
decisions.rationale: ReleaseSafe enables runtime bounds checking, overflow detection, and undefined detection while maintaining good performance. Unlike Rust, Zig does not prevent use-after-free or iterator invalidation at compile time — safety relies on runtime checks and disciplined coding.
decisions.details:
Safety modes:
  Debug — all safety checks, fills 0xAA for uninitialized memory
  ReleaseSafe — all safety checks with optimizations (recommended for production)
  ReleaseFast — no safety checks (performance-critical only)
  ReleaseSmall — no safety checks (size-constrained only)

defer/errdefer patterns:
- defer allocator.free(memory) — cleanup always runs
- errdefer allocator.free(memory) — cleanup only on error return
- defer assert(postcondition) — verify state at scope exit
- errdefer |err| log.err("failed: {}", .{err}) — log at origin

Use wrapping (+%, -%, *%) and saturating (+|, -|, *|) arithmetic for intentional overflow.

Use DebugAllocator during development to catch UAF and double-free at runtime.

decisions.decision: Always run zig fmt; trailing commas control single-line vs multi-line layout
decisions.rationale: zig fmt is the canonical AST-based auto-formatter (like gofmt). Zero configuration. All Zig code should be formatted with it. Non-canonical formatting causes unnecessary churn in diffs.
decisions.details:
Run: zig fmt src/ or zig fmt . to format all Zig files.

Trailing commas are significant: a trailing comma forces multi-line layout. Removing the trailing comma collapses to single-line if it fits.

Use // zig fmt: off and // zig fmt: on directives to opt out for specific blocks.

Enforce in CI: zig fmt --check src/ (non-zero exit on diff).

decisions.decision: Use build.zig with the module-based API; define standard steps for run, test, and install
decisions.rationale: build.zig is Zig code — not a DSL — providing full expressiveness. The module-based API (createModule) is the current standard. Steps run concurrently as a DAG; dependency edges determine execution order.

decisions.decision: Document all public APIs with /// doc comments; use //! for module-level documentation; write doctests
decisions.rationale: Zig doc comments support Markdown and are rendered by autodoc. Doctests (test named with an identifier) appear in generated documentation and serve as both docs and tests.
decisions.details:
/// for declaration docs (the item below). //! for module/file-level docs (at file top).

Doctests: test myFunction { ... } — named with an identifier, appears in docs for myFunction.

Doc comments support Markdown formatting. No HTML tags allowed — only Markdown that looks good both as source and rendered.

Generate docs: via build system or zig build-exe -femit-docs.
